title: What Operations Management Is
simple_html:
Operations management (OM) is the part of a business that actually makes the product or delivers the service. Marketing wins the customer and finance funds the firm, but operations is where the work gets done: turning inputs into the goods and services people pay for.

The core idea

Every operation can be seen as a transformation process: it takes inputs (materials, people, information, equipment) and turns them into outputs (finished products or completed services). A bakery takes flour, ovens, and bakers and produces bread. A hospital takes patients, doctors, and equipment and produces treated, healthier people.

Why it matters

Operations is usually where most of a company's costs sit and where most of its staff work. If operations runs well, the firm delivers good quality, on time, at a low cost. If it runs badly, no amount of clever marketing will save it. Done brilliantly, operations becomes a competitive weapon (think of how reliably Toyota builds cars or how fast Amazon ships).

Key takeaways

  • OM is universal: factories, hospitals, banks, airlines, and software firms all have operations.
  • Inputs to outputs: the central job is transforming resources into value for customers.
  • Goods vs services: services are often intangible, produced and consumed at the same time, and harder to store.
  • It's strategic: operations can lower cost and raise quality, speed, and reliability at once.
  • It connects everything: operations links suppliers, the firm, and customers into one flow.

In short, operations management is the discipline of designing, running, and improving the systems that create and deliver a company's products and services.
intermediate_html:
Operations management (OM) is the activity of managing the resources and processes that produce and deliver an organisation's products and services. It sits alongside marketing and finance as one of the three core functions of any business, and it is typically the largest in terms of people and cost.

The Input-Transformation-Output (ITO) model

The foundational way to view any operation is as a transformation system. Inputs fall into two groups: transformed resources (the things that get changed: materials, information, or customers) and transforming resources (the things that do the changing: facilities and staff). The transformation process acts on the transformed resources to create outputs: goods, services, or both.

Operations differ in what they transform:

  • Materials processing (a factory changes physical properties; a warehouse changes location).
  • Information processing (an accountant or bank changes the form/ownership of information).
  • Customer processing (a hairdresser changes physical state; an airline changes location; a hospital changes physiological state).

Goods versus services

Most operations produce a mix of both. The classic distinguishing features of services are often summarised as IHIP: Intangibility (you can't touch it), Heterogeneity (each delivery varies), Inseparability (produced and consumed simultaneously), and Perishability (you can't stock-pile it). A restaurant meal blends a tangible good (the food) with an intangible service (the experience), so most offerings sit on a goods-service continuum rather than at a pure extreme.

The hierarchy of operations

Operations exist at three levels, all using the same ITO logic: the supply network (the firm plus its suppliers and customers), the operation (the whole business), and the process (an individual activity within it). A useful insight is that every department is an internal operation with its own suppliers and customers, so OM thinking applies everywhere, not just on the factory floor.

Worked example: a coffee shop

Consider a single coffee shop. Transformed resources: coffee beans, milk, and the customers themselves. Transforming resources: the espresso machine, the premises, and the baristas. Process: taking the order, grinding and brewing, serving. Outputs: a drink (good) and a pleasant, fast experience (service). A manager who understands this map can see exactly where to act: a slow espresso machine is a transforming-resource constraint; inconsistent bean quality is a transformed-resource input problem.

How to apply it

To analyse any business with the ITO model: (1) list the inputs and split them into transformed vs transforming, (2) describe the transformation, (3) define the outputs and who values them, then (4) ask where cost, quality, or speed is being won or lost. This single map is the entry point to almost every other OM tool. It tells you what kind of operation you are running, which in turn shapes how you should design processes, plan capacity, and compete, the subjects of the lessons that follow.

Why it is strategic

Because operations command the bulk of a firm's assets and staff, small efficiency or quality gains scale into large competitive advantages. Firms like Toyota, IKEA, and Amazon are operations-led: their advantage is not a single product but a superior way of producing and delivering, which rivals find very hard to copy.
full_html:
Operations management (OM) is the design, planning, control, and improvement of the systems that create and deliver an organisation's products and services. It is one of the three core functions of any enterprise, alongside marketing (which generates demand) and finance/accounting (which secures and tracks funds), and it is almost always the function that employs the most people and consumes the most assets. This lesson establishes what OM is, the model that underpins the entire discipline, and why operations decisions are inherently strategic.

Defining the field

Slack, Brandon-Jones and Johnston (2019) define operations management as "the activity of managing the resources that produce and deliver products and services." Chase and colleagues frame it as the design and management of transformation processes. The common thread is transformation: OM is fundamentally about converting resources into value. Crucially, the distinction between "operations" (the function) and "operations management" (the task of running it) matters; and the related field of operations research (Morse and Kimball, 1951) supplies many of the quantitative tools OM uses, but is not synonymous with it.

The Input-Transformation-Output model

The organising framework of OM is the Input-Transformation-Output (ITO) model. Inputs divide into transformed resources, the materials, information, or customers that are changed, and transforming resources, the facilities and staff that do the changing. The transformation acts on the transformed resources to yield outputs of goods and services, and feedback from outputs informs control of future inputs.

Operations are commonly classified by their dominant transformed resource:

  • Materials processors alter physical properties (manufacturing), location (logistics), ownership (retail), or storage (warehousing).
  • Information processors alter the form (accountants), possession (market research), or location (telecoms) of information.
  • Customer processors alter physical state (hairdressers), physiological state (hospitals), location (airlines), or psychological state (entertainment).

This typology matters because it shapes managerial priorities: customer-processing operations must manage the customer's presence and perceptions in real time, whereas materials processors can buffer with inventory and decouple production from consumption.

The goods-service continuum

Early literature treated goods and services as distinct, summarising service characteristics as IHIP: intangibility, heterogeneity, inseparability of production and consumption, and perishability. Modern thinking, notably the service-dominant logic of Vargo and Lusch (2004), argues that all economic exchange is ultimately about service (value-in-use) and that goods are simply a distribution mechanism for that service. In practice almost every offering is a bundle, so managers locate it on a goods-service continuum rather than forcing a binary classification. Theodore Levitt's (1972) argument that "everybody is in service" anticipated this view and pushed manufacturers to apply service-quality thinking to their products.

The hierarchy of operations and internal customers

The ITO logic is fractal: it applies at three nested levels. The supply network level views the firm within its web of suppliers and customers; the operation level views the whole business as one transformation system; and the process level views any single activity within it. A key managerial insight is the internal customer-supplier concept: every department both receives from internal suppliers and serves internal customers, so OM discipline applies to HR, IT, and finance just as much as to the factory floor. This idea is the conceptual bridge to process design (see Lesson 4) and to supply chain management (Lesson 6).

The transformation of the field: from cost to capability

OM's intellectual history frames why it is strategic today. Scientific management (Taylor, 1911) and the moving assembly line (Ford, c. 1913) made operations a source of unprecedented productivity but treated it narrowly as cost minimisation. The post-war Japanese quality movement, shaped by Deming (1986) and Juran, and crystallised in the Toyota Production System (Ohno, 1988), reframed operations as a source of both low cost and high quality, dissolving the assumed trade-off. Skinner's (1969) landmark paper "Manufacturing, missing link in corporate strategy" argued that operations had been neglected strategically and that a firm's operating system must be deliberately aligned with its competitive priorities, the foundation of operations strategy (Lesson 3). Hayes and Wheelwright (1984) extended this by showing operations could move from a passive, internally neutral role to an "externally supportive" stage where it actively drives competitive advantage.

Worked example

Map a hospital emergency department with the ITO model. Transformed resources: patients (the dominant one), plus pharmaceuticals and clinical information. Transforming resources: doctors, nurses, diagnostic equipment, the building. Process: triage, diagnosis, treatment, discharge. Outputs: treated patients (changed physiological state) and an experience (reassurance, dignity). Because the dominant transformed resource is the customer, the ED cannot hold "finished inventory"; demand variability hits it directly, perishability and inseparability dominate, and quality is judged partly on the experience, not only the clinical outcome. This single map immediately tells a manager that capacity buffering and queue management (Lesson 5) will be central concerns, illustrating how the foundational model drives every downstream decision.

Common misconceptions

  • "OM is just manufacturing." In advanced economies the majority of operations are services; the ITO model applies identically to both.
  • "Operations is purely tactical/cost-focused." Since Skinner (1969), operations is understood as a strategic capability, not merely an efficiency engine.
  • "Quality and cost trade off." The Japanese quality revolution showed that better quality often reduces cost by eliminating rework and waste (a theme developed in Lessons 7 and 8).
  • "Operations is the back office." Via the internal-customer concept, every function is an operation.

Cross-links

This lesson is the gateway to the course. It feeds directly into the 4 Vs and performance objectives (Lesson 2), which describe how operations differ and what they are judged on; operations strategy (Lesson 3), which aligns the transformation system with competitive goals; and process design (Lesson 4), which applies the ITO model at the process level. Master the transformation model here and the rest of the syllabus becomes a structured elaboration of it.
